---
title: 'Fencing Swords'
date: '2015-12-19T15:19:00.0010000+00:00'
draft: true
blogger_blog: 'Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller'
blogger_status: 'LIVE'
blogger_post_id: '605502783201170095'
blogger_filename: '/2015/12/fencing-swords_19.html'
blogger_source: 'takeout-20260223T235336Z-3-001.zip:Takeout/Blogger/Blogs/Darrell Wolfe, Storyteller/feed.atom'
categories:
  - 'Research'
---

<br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><div style="border-width: 100%; direction: ltr;"><br /><br /><div style="direction: ltr; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; width: 7.8652in;"><br /><br /><div style="direction: ltr; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; width: 7.8652in;"><br /><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: .2902in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;"><br /> <div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="color: #265e15; font-family: Georgia; font-size: 10.0pt; line-height: 14pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">So what about Rapiers?</span></div><br /><div style="color: #333333; font-family: verdana; font-size: 9.1pt; line-height: 14pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">You may also see references to<br /> Rapier swords. These are the heavy, original 16th century swords that Foil and<br /> Epee swords were based on.</span></div><br /><div style="color: #333333; font-family: verdana; font-size: 9.1pt; line-height: 14pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">So most of the Rapiers being<br /> sold are reproductions for collectors and for use in re-enactments. Probably<br /> not something you would actually want to fence with!</span></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="color: #595959; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 9.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br />From<br /> &lt;<a href="https://fencingswords.wordpress.com/">https://fencingswords.wordpress.com/</a>&gt;<br /> </div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">Weapons</span><span style="background: white; color: #555555;">[</span><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Fencing&amp;action=edit&amp;section=6"><span style="background: white;">edit</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #555555;">]</span></div><br /><div style="color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt; line-height: 16pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">There are three weapons in<br /> modern fencing: foil, épée, and sabre. Each weapon has its own rules and<br /> strategies.</span></div><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Foil_(fencing)"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Foil</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: a light thrusting weapon, with a maximum weight of 500 grams.<br />      The foil may target the&nbsp;</span><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Torso"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">torso</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">&nbsp;(including the back), neck, and groin, but not the arms or<br />      legs. The foil has a small circular hand guard that serves to protect the<br />      hand from direct stabs. As the hand is not a valid target in foil, this<br />      is primarily for safety. Touches are scored only with the tip; hits with<br />      the side of the blade do not count, and do not halt the action. Touches<br />      that land outside of the target area (called an&nbsp;</span><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt; font-style: italic;">off-target touch</span><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">) stop the action, but<br />      are not scored. Only a single touch can be scored by either fencer at one<br />      time. If both fencers land valid touches at the same time, the&nbsp;</span><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Referee"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">referee</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">&nbsp;uses the rules of "right of way" to determine<br />      which fencer gets the point. If both fencers begin their attack at the<br />      same time, or the referee is unable to determine who was first, neither<br />      fencer scores a point.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C3%89p%C3%A9e"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Épée</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: a thrusting weapon like the foil, but heavier, with a maximum<br />      total weight of 770 grams. In épée, the entire body is valid target. The<br />      hand guard on the épée is a large circle that extends towards the pommel,<br />      effectively covering the hand, which is a valid target in épée. Like<br />      foil, all hits must be with the tip and not the sides of the blade. Hits<br />      with the side of the blade do not halt the action. As the entire body is<br />      legal target, there is not the concept of an off-target touch, except if<br />      the fencer accidentally strikes the floor, setting off the electric tone.<br />      Unlike foil and sabre, épée does not use "right of way", and<br />      allows simultaneous hits by both fencers. However, if the score is tied<br />      in a match at the last point and a double touch is scored, the point is<br />      null and void.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sabre_(fencing)"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Sabre</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: a light cutting and thrusting weapon that targets the entire<br />      body above the waist, except the weapon hand. Like the foil, the maximum<br />      legal weight of a sabre is 500 grams. The hand guard on the sabre extends<br />      from pommel to the base of where the blade connects to the hilt. This<br />      guard is generally turned outwards during sport to protect the sword arm<br />      from touches. Hits with the entire blade or point are valid. As in foil,<br />      touches that land outside of the target area are not scored. However,<br />      unlike foil, these&nbsp;</span><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt; font-style: italic;">off-target</span><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">&nbsp;touches do not stop the action, and the fencing continues.<br />      In the case of both fencers landing a scoring touch, the referee<br />      determines which fencer receives the point for the action, again through<br />      the use of "right of way".</span></li><br /></ul><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="color: #595959; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 9.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br />From<br /> &lt;<a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fencing#Weapons">https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fencing#Weapons</a>&gt;<br /> </div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt; line-height: 16pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><span style="background: white;">Techniques or movements in<br /> fencing can be divided into two categories: offensive and quickly defensive.<br /> Some techniques can fall into both categories (</span><span style="background: white; font-style: italic;">e.g.</span><span style="background: white;">&nbsp;the<br /> beat). Certain techniques are used offensively, with the purpose of landing a<br /> hit on your opponent while holding the right of way (foil and sabre). Others<br /> are used defensively, to protect against a hit or obtain the right of way.</span></div><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Offensive</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Attack_(fencing)"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Attack</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: A basic fencing technique, also called a thrust, consisting of<br />      extending the sword arm to declare an attack and attempt to land a touch<br />      upon the opponent's valid area. In sabre, attacks are also made with a<br />      cutting action.</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Riposte: An<br />      attack by the defender after a successful parry. After the attacker has<br />      completed their attack, and it has been parried, the defender then has<br />      the opportunity to make an attack, and take right of way (foil and<br />      saber).</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Feint"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Feint</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: An attack with the purpose of provoking a reaction from the<br />      opposing fencer.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Lunge_(fencing)"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Lunge</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: A thrust while extending the front leg by using a slight kicking<br />      motion and propelling the body forward with the back leg.</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Beat<br />      Attack: In foil &amp; sabre, the attacker beats the opponent's blade to<br />      gain priority (right of way) and continues the attack against the target<br />      area. In épée, a similar beat is made but with the intention to disturb<br />      the opponent's aim and thus score with a single light.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Disengage:<br />      Beginning an attack in one direction, then quickly moving the point down<br />      in a semi-circle to attack a different location. This is used to trick<br />      the opponent into blocking the wrong direction. For example, the fencer<br />      could target the left side of the torso, and begin the lunge. As the<br />      opponent moves to the left to parry, the fencer disengages and finishes<br />      the attack on the right side of the torso. Commonly countered with a<br />      circle-parry.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Continuation<br />      of Attack: A typical épée action of making a 2nd after attack after the<br />      first attack is parried. This may be done with a change in line; example,<br />      an attack in the high line (above the opponent's bellguard, such as the<br />      shoulder) is then followed with an attack to the low line (below the<br />      opponent's bellguard, such as the thigh, or foot); or from the outside<br />      line (outside of the bellguard, such as outer arm) to the inside line<br />      (inside the bellguard, such as the inner arm or the chest). A second<br />      continuation is stepping slight past the parry and angulating the blade<br />      to bring the tip of the blade back on target.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Remise. A<br />      second attack immediately after the first has missed or been parried. In<br />      foil or sabre, a remise is considered to have lost right of way, and the<br />      defender's riposte will always score instead of the remise.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Flick: a<br />      technique used primarily in foil. It takes advantage of the extreme<br />      flexibility of the blade to use it like a whip, bending the blade so that<br />      it curves over and strikes the opponent with the point. This technique<br />      has become much more difficult due to timing changes which require the<br />      point to stay depressed for longer to set off the light.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Defensive</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #0b0080; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Parry_(fencing)"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Parry</span></a><span style="background: white; color: #252525; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">: Basic defence technique, block the opponent's weapon while it is<br />      preparing or executing an attack to deflect the blade away from the<br />      fencer's valid area and (in foil and sabre) to give fencer the right of<br />      way. Usually followed by a riposte, a return attack by defender.</span></li><br /></ul><br /><ul style="direction: ltr; margin-bottom: 0in; margin-left: 0in; margin-top: 0in; unicode-bidi: embed;" type="disc"><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Circle<br />      Parry: A parry where the sword is twisted in a circle to catch the<br />      opponent's tip and deflect it away. It is commonly used to counter a<br />      disengage.</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Counter<br />      Attack: A basic fencing technique of attacking your opponent while<br />      generally moving back out of the way of the opponent's attack. Used quite<br />      often in épée to score against the attacker's hand/arm. More difficult to<br />      accomplish in foil and sabre unless one is quick enough to make the<br />      counterattack and retreat ahead of the advancing opponent without being<br />      scored upon, or by evading the attacking blade via moves such as the In<br />      Quartata (turning to the side) or Passata-sotto (ducking).</span></li><br /><li style="color: #252525; margin-bottom: 0; margin-top: 0; vertical-align: middle;"><span style="background: white; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 10.5pt;">Point In<br />      Line: Extending the weapon and arm against the opponent's target area. In<br />      foil and sabre, this gives one priority if the extension is made before<br />      the opponent is approximately advance-lunge distance away. When performed<br />      as a defensive action, the attacker must then disturb the extended weapon<br />      to re-take priority; otherwise the defender has priority and the<br />      counter-attack like action will win the touch if the attacker does not<br />      manage a single light. When performed as an offensive action, the intent<br />      is usually a means for the attacker to draw a defensive action that can<br />      be deceived and the attack continued. In épée, there is no priority; the<br />      move may be used as a means by either fencer to achieve a double-touch<br />      and advance the score by 1 for each fencer.</span></li><br /></ul><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="color: #595959; font-family: Calibri; font-size: 9.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br />From<br /> &lt;<a href="https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fencing#Weapons">https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fencing#Weapons</a>&gt;<br /> </div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br /><br /></div><br /><div style="font-family: Calibri; font-size: 11.0pt; margin: 0in;"><br />**</div><br /></ul><br /></div><br /></div><br /></div><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br /><br />
